serving.description: 1 plate (rice set with beverage and side)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 450 g, comprising a plate of steamed white rice topped with salted egg minced pork, served with one large beverage and one side dish.

nutrient_source.title: Salted Egg Minced Pork Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Salted Egg Minced Pork Rice, as recorded in the input record with provenance 'canonical' and confidence 0.95. Saturated fat data not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central pork and salted egg component data and documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Salted Egg Minced Pork Rice as a composite dish. GI of 72 is estimated using white rice (jasmine/steamed) as the dominant carbohydrate vehicle, which has a published GI of approximately 72 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). The minced pork and salted egg components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI of the dish. Glycemic load calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 25.19 × 4.5) / 100 ≈ 40.8 per 450 g serving. This is a conservative estimate; actual GL may be higher depending on rice portion size.

hypertension.key_factor: High effective sodium load from salted duck egg and condiments
hypertension.guidance: Although the composite per-100g sodium is 231 mg, salted duck egg alone contains approximately 1,000–1,500 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish, request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce, and verify actual sodium per full serving before consumption.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base driving rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: The white rice base carries a GI of approximately 72 and delivers approximately 113 g of carbohydrate per 450 g serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request brown rice or a reduced rice portion, monitor total carbohydrate intake per meal, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from pork and salted egg yolk combined with high sodium load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat from minced pork and salted egg yolk, combined with the high effective sodium from the salted egg and condiments, elevates cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, choose leaner protein alternatives, and request reduced-sodium condiments.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid from pork and high sodium from salted egg worsening tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Minced pork is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6) which amplifies inflammatory cytokines, and salted egg contributes very high sodium that worsens RA-related tissue swelling; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting pork with chicken or tofu, limit salted egg to half or less per serving, and increase the vegetable portion to improve the anti-inflammatory balance.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salted Egg Minced Pork Rice Set (咸蛋肉碎饭套餐) is a popular Singaporean hawker and casual-dining dish consisting of steamed white jasmine rice topped with a savoury stir-fried or braised minced pork mixture cooked with salted duck egg, garlic, soy sauce, and oyster sauce. The salted egg yolk imparts a rich, creamy, and intensely savoury flavour with a slightly grainy texture, while the minced pork provides a tender, moist protein base. The dish is typically served as a set meal with one large beverage and one side dish such as stir-fried vegetables or soup. Nutritionally, the dish provides 164 kcal per 100 g, with 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate. The white rice base carries a high glycaemic index of approximately 72, making the dish a high-glycaemic-load meal at approximately 40.8 GL per 450 g serving. Sodium content is a significant clinical concern: while the composite figure reads 231 mg per 100 g, salted duck egg alone contains 1,000–1,500 mg sodium per 100 g, meaning the actual sodium per full serving is substantially higher. The dish is not suitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or rheumatoid arthritis without significant modification. It is a staple lunch and dinner option across Singapore's hawker centres and food courts, enjoyed by Chinese, Malay, and other communities.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with provenance marked 'canonical' and confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and documented. GI estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using white rice as the dominant carbohydrate vehicle, which is a well-established and defensible analogous estimate.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.4 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for ground pork (USDA FoodData Central #168351) and salted duck egg components, as HPB does not report saturated fat for this composite entry. This is an approximation and should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, fat-soluble vitamins, B-vitamin panel, and moisture data for this composite Singaporean hawker dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source record. This is flagged as potentially anomalous given that salted duck egg yolk is known to be high in cholesterol (approximately 680 mg/100g for egg yolk); the composite 0.0 value may reflect a data entry limitation in the HPB record. Users should treat the cholesterol figure with caution and consult a dietitian for clinical decisions. 4. The dish is not halal-certified as it contains pork. 5. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on steamed white jasmine rice as the dominant carbohydrate vehicle; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2026. 6. Sodium per full serving is likely significantly higher than the composite per-100g figure suggests due to the high sodium content of salted duck egg.
